title: Generalized continuum theory of phonon angular momentum in crystals
authors: Mamoru Matsuo, Naoki Nishimura, Ai Yamakage, Takeo Kato
abstract: We formulate a generalized continuum theory of phonon angular momentum in crystals by introducing a local SO(3) material frame in addition to the macroscopic displacement field. The local frame represents rotational optical degrees of freedom of the unit cell and brings acoustic displacement modes and optical rotational modes into a common long-wavelength continuum description. In the linearized limit, the co-rotated deformation gradient and the rotational gradient associated with the local material frame recover the Eringen microdeformation and wryness tensors; isotropic micropolar elasticity then appears as a special case. Rotational symmetry and Noether's theorem determine the continuum phonon angular-momentum density, including both the displacement-polarization contribution and the intrinsic microrotation contribution. The theory further identifies the locking limit in which microrotation reduces to lattice vorticity and the improper-symmetry-breaking terms responsible for chiral phonon splitting.
summary: This paper establishes a new continuum theory for phonon angular momentum that bridges the gap between acoustic and optical phonons. By introducing a local rotation frame, it provides a consistent way to calculate angular momentum density and explains how structural chirality leads to the splitting of phonon modes.
main_problem: The lack of a unified continuum theory that can simultaneously describe both acoustic displacement modes and optical rotational modes within a single conservation law for phonon angular momentum.
main_result: The development of a generalized continuum theory using a local SO(3) material frame that identifies a definite phonon angular-momentum density and explains chiral phonon splitting.
method: The authors use a local-frame formulation of micropolar elasticity, applying Noether's theorem to a Lagrangian density to derive conservation laws and analyzing the linearized limit and symmetry-breaking terms.
model_or_system: A generalized continuum model of crystals/solids, specifically focusing on microstructures with rotational optical degrees of freedom represented by a local SO(3) material frame.
key_observables: Phonon angular momentum density (comprising orbital, displacement-polarization, and intrinsic microrotation parts), phonon dispersion relations, and chiral splitting of modes.
important_parameters: Mass density, moment of inertia, Lamé parameters, Cosserat/wryness moduli, and the chiral coupling constant.
main_assumptions: The theory is applied in the long-wavelength (continuum) limit and assumes a regime where torsion and curvature terms are not required; the electron-spin coupling is deferred to future work.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a 2D schematic of microrotation and displacement fields in a lattice; Figure 2 shows phonon dispersion relations; Figure 3 compares transverse phonon dispersions in achiral versus chiral media.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of unified phonon angular momentum, defines the local SO(3) material frame and co-rotated deformation gradient, applies Noether's theorem to derive angular momentum density, analyzes the linearized limit (micropolar elasticity), explores the locking limit and chiral splitting, and concludes with technical remarks on the hierarchy of continua.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 2. Schematic of the phonon dispersion relations in the long-wavelength limit for transverse phonons in (a) achiral materials and (b) chiral materials. A pair of adjacent lines in (a) indicates the two-fold degeneracy of phonon modes. The magnitude of the frequency splitting in (b) is proportional to k for optical phonons and to k2 for acoustic phonons.

Figure 1. Two-dimensional schematic of the microrotation field ϕ and the displacement field u in a continuum medium. The square mesh indicates the atomic displacement described by the field u, accompanied by a local rotation in the x1-x2 plane, whereas the ellipse denotes the rotation of the internal degree. Here, a is a lattice constant and ei (i = 1, 2) are unit vectors.

Figure 3. Transverse phonon dispersions in micropolar elas- ticity. In the achiral case, the acoustic and optical branches remain degenerate, whereas in the chiral case, the optical branch is split and the acoustic branch exhibits a weaker split- ting. The curves are plotted for representative dimensionless parameters ρ = 1, I = 0.5, µ = 1, κ = 0.5, and β + γ = 1. The achiral panel corresponds to χ = 0, whereas the chiral panel uses χ = 0.2 in the convention of Eq. (57). No material- specific fit is implied.
